title: Using diluted urine as a compost nitrogen activator
subtitle: A free, effective nitrogen source to speed a slow or carbon-heavy heap
excerpt: Human urine is a balanced nitrogen source with a carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of around 1:1, making it one of the most effective free activators for a slow or carbon-heavy compost heap. Diluted 10:1 with water and watered into the heap at the centre, it provides immediately available nitrogen without the risk of burning. This is when to use it, how to dilute it, and how much to apply.
sourceNotes: Richert A et al, 'Practical Guidance on the Use of Urine in Crop Production' (Stockholm Environment Institute, 2010, open access). Garden Organic 'Compost activators' factsheet. WRAP Home Composting study appendices on nitrogen activators.

glossaryTerms.definition: A nitrogen-rich material added to a compost heap to lower the overall C:N ratio and stimulate microbial activity. Common nitrogen activators include grass clippings, food scraps, blood meal, poultry manure, and diluted urine.

The ratio of carbon to nitrogen in a composting material. Microbes need roughly 25 to 30 parts carbon for every one part nitrogen. A ratio above 30:1 indicates a slow, nitrogen-deficient heap; adding nitrogen activators brings it back into range.

content.text: A heap dominated by wood chip, straw, autumn leaves, or shredded paper decomposes slowly because the

is too high for microbes to work. The usual fix is adding green materials (grass clippings, food scraps), but fresh urine works at once and is free. Urine converts quickly to ammonium, an available nitrogen form. Its C:N ratio is roughly 1:1, making it an effective
Signs that a heap needs nitrogen
The heap is unchanged in volume after two months with no visible decomposition
The contents look pale grey or brown and dry, not dark
A large batch of wood chip, cardboard, or straw was added without balancing nitrogen

Collect fresh urine in a container. It does not need to be morning urine; any time works. Use urine from a person who is well-hydrated (pale yellow); very concentrated dark urine has a higher salt content that can slow the heap slightly.
Dilute 1 part urine to 10 parts water (1 cup urine in 10 cups water). This reduces salt concentration and allows the nitrogen to spread through the heap without creating hot spots.

Make a hollow in the centre of the heap and pour in the diluted solution slowly. Cover with a handful of carbon material (wood chip or cardboard). The hollow routes the liquid to the centre where nitrogen-limited microbial activity is highest.
Repeat once a week for two to four weeks. Check after two weeks: the heap should be noticeably warmer, darker, and should have reduced in volume.

For a 1 m³ heap of high-carbon material: approximately 5 to 10 litres of diluted solution (500 ml to 1 litre of undiluted urine) per application, once a week. The response is typically visible within 10 to 14 days in warm weather.

Fresh urine from a healthy person is low-risk. WHO guidelines on urine use in agriculture confirm that heat and time in the heap eliminate pathogen risk. If taking medication, dilute 20:1 instead of 10:1; most pharmaceuticals degrade within weeks in a warm heap. This practice is common in permaculture and allotment growing.
